page.content: The following table presents a comprehensive analysis of the Body Mass Index (BMI) disparities between male and female subjects, with a focus on the associated psychological distress as measured by the 4DSQ-scales. The inclusion of metrics such as the p-values for both genders emphasizes the statistical significance of the observed differences in BMI. Notably, the mean BMI for males was recorded at -0.051 with a p-value of < 0.0001, indicating a robust correlation with psychological distress levels. In contrast, the female subjects exhibited a mean BMI of -0.014, also with a p-value of < 0.0001, further reinforcing the link between body weight and mental health outcomes across genders. The 4DSQ-scales, which range from 0 to 32, provide a standardized measure of distress, allowing for a nuanced understanding of how BMI impacts psychological well-being.

page.content: Furthermore, the comparative analysis between Turkish and Dutch populations offers valuable insights into cultural variations in BMI and distress, as indicated by the respective columns.

page.content: This data is critical for informing targeted interventions aimed at addressing mental health issues related to obesity and body image across diverse populations.
